speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 16th, 1996. This interview is with George Schillinger. The interviewer is Burton Leiser. The interview is taking place in Scarsdale, New York, USA. And the language of the interview will be English. Today is August 16th, 1996. This interview is with George Schillinger. I am Burton Leiser-- B-U-R-T-O-N L-E-I-S-E-R. We are in Scarsdale, New York, USA. And the interview will take place in English. Please state your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is George Schillinger, S-C-H-I-L-L-I-N-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name in Hebrew is [? Jacov ?] [? ben-Eliochum. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in April 10th, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Szeghalom, S-Z-E-G-H-A-L-O-M, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about your father? What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's name was Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he born and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Gyula, G-Y-U-L-A, in Hungary, September 30, 1891.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, Yolanda Diamond, or [? Diamond. ?] She was born in April the 2nd, 102 in Nyitra, N-Y-I-T-R-A, in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one sister named Eva. And she was born in January 30, 1925, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Szeghalom.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your aunts and uncles? Can you tell us about aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had six sisters and brothers who lived also in the same area, actually. Would you like to know the names?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: The names was Lipot, L-I-P-O-T, Margit, M-A-R-G-I-T, Julia, Aladar, A-L-A-D-A-R, Armin, and Dezso, D-E-Z-S-0 with an umlaut.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any on your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had sisters in the-- and one brother in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met them after the war when we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And you, you are married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am married. My wife's name is Zahava. And I have three sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don, Dean, and Ben.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you know your grandparents at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never met your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never met any of my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your address in Szeghalom?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on Main Street, 33, in Szeghalom. And before that, I think in my early childhood, we lived on a street called Simay, S-I-M-A-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall of your childhood? What are your earliest memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember back to age four. We lived in a small town, Szeghalom, as I mentioned. It had a population of 10,000. It was about 30 kilometers from the Romanian border in East Hungary on the plains. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember from very early childhood a, a very warm, very tight-knit family and family life, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] many friends. And even in my earliest years, I was very conscious of, of being a Jew and treated differently in that little town, which was actually rather liberal. And the behavior in general would be quite acceptable, even if-- with today's eyes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they made us feel, very early, that we were outsiders.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you had a very warm and close-knit family, can you recall any specific incidents or things that, that, that stand out in your memory that illustrate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father had a, a large store, a hardware store. And he really spent all his life with us, with the family. He never went on a vacation. We were always together. My mother was the key to our social life. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] religious holidays were kept regularly. And it was not an extremely religious family, though. But traditional evenings like Seder and, and the high holidays were celebrated at home in, in a very Jewish manner. However, my mother came from Czechoslovakia from a gentry family. And she was not so familiar with the Jewish customs as my father, who came from an Orthodox family. But the atmosphere was always very warm and caring and, and loving. And, and I remember every day with my parents with the utmost love and respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example, can you recall what it was like when-- on a typical day when you were going off to school? What would-- how would the morning begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rising was not easy. I never liked to get up in the morning. My mother would wake me up. And she would dress me. And she would give me breakfast. And we would walk, walk to school. Because in that little town, everything was close by. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How close was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say the elementary school was maybe two, three kilometers, which was a walking distance, even in our age. And that was a, a Jewish school that I went to. That's where I learned the Hebrew letters. All my friends attended the school. And I had a wonderful teacher whose picture I'll be able to show you later. He was bright. He was enthusiastic. He loved us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what gave us actually-- he survived the war, even this particular teacher, and became a very famous doctor in Norway. He, he became a cancer researcher of well-known quality.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of breakfast would your mother prepare for you in those days? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Breakfast was simple in, in Hungary, at least in my town. It was coffee with milk. Of course, not real coffee-- it was chicory with a lot of milk and bread, jam, butter, never any meat products in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she prepare you a lunch to go to school with, or did you come back home for lunch?

speaker: interviewee
text: We usually, in the early times, we would come back. When I was in high school, I had a, a lunch prepared, a sandwich prepared, which I promptly traded for stamps.

speaker: interviewer
text: For stamps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explained that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was an avid stamp collector. And by all means, I, I would have enhanced my collection. So I traded many times my sandwiches. And I was a very skinny little guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just sticking with your memories of family life a little bit, when you came home from school, what would you find? What would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother worked in the store with my father. So I was left to my means. And our housekeeper, whose name was Marcella-- I will show her picture, too-- she took care of me. And we had a lovely home, actually one of the few high-rise buildings in town on the Main Street above my father's store. And my early childhood was extremely joyful because of many friends and, and the adventures in my father's stores. Because he, he sold motorcycles, bicycles, agriculture machinery, guns. And I was free to roam in, in the stores, in the storage places. And, and my friends would come there. And they were always welcomed in our house. And they were fed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever work in your dad's store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever work in your father's store, or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did later, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I sold behind the counter. And sometimes I, I was at the cashiers and took money-- but I was a little older-- and freely dipped for chocolate in the, in the counter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of relationship did you have with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very warm, very respectful-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he never hit me. He never talked to me ever [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with a raised voice. He never said a bad word. That was taboo in our house. There was no, no freedom in that respect. And I never heard him saying anything. I never heard him fighting either with my mother. If should, should be angry at me at, in any time, he would just be very silent and pale. And I, I was far more afraid of him at that time than, than if he had hit me or anything else-- was extremely warm-hearted, a good man. We always said that he is the heart of the Schillingers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a extremely popular man in town-- and everybody, Christians and Jews. And and later on, my friends, when they came to visit me, they always said that, you know, your store is not just a store. Your father's store is a, is a locale. It, it-- I heard farmers talking that where you going to meet you? I said, I meet you at Schillingers. It was a very prominent place. And my father was extremely diligent.

speaker: interviewee
text: My relation was warm and correct and trustful. When I had my little secrets and, and little problems to discuss, I would go to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your relations with your mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was my confidant. She would take me for walks. Main Street was lovely, especially in the evenings. We had acacia trees. And, and my mother liked to walk every evening. And she would take me for her walks. We would take the dog with her. Even the cat came with us sometimes and accompanied. And we discussed the world and problems. And she was extremely colorful person. I don't know if with any interest if I describe her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a, an extremely high education. She spoke many languages-- French, English, German, Czech, Slovak, Hungaian-- unfortunately, never learned any Hebrew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Later on, when I learned myself, I remember this. This is-- I'm filling in a gap in my mother's education. And she played piano very good, classical piano. Extremely energetic woman-- she played tennis and swam. And she belonged actually to a, a club, a gentry club where Jews were not very welcome in, in my little town. And that was the only place they had a tennis court where I picked balls for, for them. But she was welcome, because she was a good tennis player. Her social life was extremely intense. She organized evenings and dinners, where they were literally evenings where they would read poems. And she played bridge sometimes in the late night when I was mad because I, I couldn't sleep as a little child. They were fighting in the other room on the bridge game.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I would say especially in those times, for a woman she, she could have said a Renaissance woman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were a very loving couple. And as I said, I, I really never recall any fights. I'm very fortunate to say that. So my childhood was peaceful and home. And, and I brought these memories to my own family. And you said that your father had been brought up in an Orthodox family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, I guess, had no particular connection with religion at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: From what you say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they resolve that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father yielded in that respect, I assume. He, he did not demand a, a kosher home. My mother was willing to, to run the house in a traditional way otherwise. As I mentioned, the, the holidays were kept. And, and we would go to synagogue. Even And she would. And she would read in Hungarian up in the balcony where the women were. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was never any conflict. in that respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do-- what synagogue did your parents attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- I would call in this country-- conservative synagogue. It was not Orthodox. But [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] maybe in this country, in the USA, it would be considered to be an Orthodox temple. Because it, it was run in that way that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the traditions were strongly enforced. They didn't have a rabbi. And there was a, a shochet [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who lived right next to the temple. And he, he kind of arranged [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the meetings. And he ran the show.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- did the synagogue have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it did not have a name. And, and I don't recall a name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your relationships with the synagogue? Did you go to a Hebrew school or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a Hebrew school for two years. And that was also next to the synagogue. And the backyard, we shared with the synagogue and also with the shochet's house. I still don't eat chicken today because of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because the shochet worked in that courtyard. And I, many times, I, I witnessed his, his job, so to speak. So we got some Jewish education there. And we learned the Hebrew letters. And we became familiar with, with the Tanakh. And my teacher, who became a doctor in Norway, was very inspiring.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the seder, let's say, on Pesach? Who would, who would come to your house usually? Or where, where did you have the seder? Was it in your home, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the seder was, was our-- in our home, yes. And it was always conducted by my father, who enjoyed this very much. And he, he, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he brought us a lot of Judaism to our house through these seders and discussions. And, and he, he opened up on those nights. And we could feel that we were really at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would ordinarily be there during the seder, just your immediate family, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Our, our, our family and, and maybe his brother and family who also lived in Szeghalom, and, and my mother's uncles maybe, and some of my friends' parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you recall the high holidays at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes the high holidays-- we never went to school. My father spent all his day at shul. And we, we had to be there also with him. We used to go out to the backyard and, and play whenever you could. And I fasted until noon. And my parents fasted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The high holidays [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] always filled, filled me with with mystery. It, it was a very mystic ritual to me to see those people in their, in their white, in their tennis shoes, or in their slippers. And then they told me that, you know, they buried the Jews also in those outfits. And it was a bit-- I was a bit fearful in those days. And I certainly prayed strong for my family always. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I, I was a believer. I still am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- how would you characterize your feelings about Judaism as a child, up, up to the time of-- when things started to get bad? Was it just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Up until that point, I. I, I, I accepted without any question. I, I enjoyed all what came with the, the, the wonderful stories from, from the Bible, the, the company, the school, the teachers, the, the learning a new language, and the traditions, and, and everything with it, up until I encountered anti-Semitism. And then the questions came, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how did anti-Semitism manifest itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, you grow up in, in a, a very chauvinistic country, like little Hungary with the national anthem, the flag. And, and you feel patriotic. You do feel-- I felt patriotic until, until they kept telling me that you're not Hungarian. You're a Jew, even then. And I, and I told, my family lived here for 300 years at least. We always felt at home, just like the German Jews. They told us that we are Germans. I always said we were Hungarians. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then more and more they told us, even as children, that you're a Jew. You're not Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then eventually when gradually things became worse and worse, when the first brutalities came, then, then you questioned, why me? Why us? Why Jews? Why, why am I different than anybody else? And why did I deserve this? And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first questions came to my mind, really. Would I like to be something else? And many times, the answer was yes. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: A child does not want to be different when you are in school and high school. You don't belong. You, you're an outsider. You suffer. They beat you. They, they exclude. They, they boycott you. You don't want to be different. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All my childhood, I wanted to be a Boy Scout. So finally, I applied. And I became a Boy Scout. And it was one of my happiest days in, in childhood. And the next day, a nice young man comes to me who was the son of the pharmacist. And he said, I have bad news for you. He said, you're out. You're fired. You're not a Boy Scout any me-- anymore, after 24 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, why? I was dreaming up, up of the uniforms and the, and the neckties, and the metals, and the camping, and-- why? Because you are a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was the first big blow that I, I received. What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- I think it was 1941, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 11, 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other incidents-- you mentioned that there were beatings and boycotts and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally experience any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me tell you about this. This town had a, a few hundred Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of them were very religious, the minority. And most of them were assimilated Jews. And I would say that we got along well with the population. And we were treated fine. And my pa-- father and my family was very popular. And his, his, his store was frequented. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a high school teacher, a the teacher of physical education. His name was Koppanyi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody contributed money to give to this man, including the Jews-- my parents and friends-- to go to Germany to see the Olympics in 1936. So Mr. Koppanyi was sent and went to Germany. He was quite a decent guy. And when he became-- pardon-- when he returned from Germany, he became a Nazi. He was so infatuated with Germany, with the Nazis, and with Hitler, and whatever he saw there, that he came back to my little town. And this man founded the Nazi party in Szeghalom, with the swastikas and uniforms and so on and so forth. So the organized anti-Semitism began in my town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop you at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll pick up here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: How, how much time will I have for, for the prewar?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll have plenty of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll have plenty of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.
